steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: The ground rules
steps.guidance: A few honest ground rules before any training. Small and gentle wins — a harder squeeze is not a better one. A pelvic floor that's always clenched causes more trouble than a weak one, so we train the release as carefully as the lift. It should never hurt: if anything causes pain, stop — that's a sign to ease off, or to check with a doctor. The 'stop your pee' idea is only for finding the muscles, not an exercise. And every session can be done sitting still, fully dressed, breathing normally — no one would notice. That's by design.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Locate
steps.guidance: We're going to find the pelvic floor. Imagine you badly need to pass gas and have to hold it in — that small internal lift. Or the muscles you'd use to stop yourself peeing midstream (only to find them — don't make a habit of it). That gentle draw up and in — that's the muscle. Keep it small. We're learning the feeling, not lifting anything heavy. And keep breathing.

steps.title: Gentle holds
steps.guidance: On your next out-breath, lift — just a little. On the in-breath, let it go. All the way, completely soft. Feel that difference: lifted... and fully let go. The letting-go is half the skill — maybe the more important half. A few of these, no rush, generous rest between each. If you can barely feel anything yet, that's completely normal — it's week one.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Full release
steps.guidance: This week is as much about letting go as lifting. Lift the floor small on the out-breath — then, on the in-breath, release it completely, all the way to soft. The release is the skill. Notice the difference between a gentle hold and a full, easy let-go. If the release feels harder than the lift, that's exactly the thing worth practising. Gentle throughout — never a forceful push.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Lengthen the out-breath
steps.guidance: Breathe in slowly through the nose for about four, letting the belly rise — then out, slow and long, for about six. Longer out than in. This calms the whole system and softens the pelvic floor with it. Keep going at your own pace; with each out-breath let a little more tension go.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Endurance holds
steps.guidance: Welcome to the build phase — the foundation was real and complete; now we load it a little. Lift small and hold for about 6 seconds, breathing the whole way, then lower in control — down in stages, never a drop. About eight, resting between. If the lift creeps bigger to keep the hold going, keep it small and let it be a shorter hold instead.

steps.title: Hold and pulse
steps.guidance: A new pattern. Lift to a comfortable base hold — about half your max — and keep it steady. On top of it, add a few quick pulses: up and back to the base, without losing the base. Pulse... pulse... pulse... the base never drops. Then lower the whole thing slowly. The base staying put while you pulse is the skill. About four, with good rests — this one's taxing.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Frame it
steps.guidance: Stop-start trains you to recognise and ride the point of no return. This is a mental rehearsal of that skill.

steps.title: Rise and pause
steps.guidance: Imagine arousal climbing. At a high point, pause and breathe until it settles. Repeat the rise and pause.

steps.title: Integrate
steps.guidance: Notice you can influence the wave. Control is a skill you’re building.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Somewhere private and unhurried.

steps.title: Attend
steps.guidance: Touch your own skin slowly, with curiosity not goal. Notice sensation for its own sake.

steps.title: Close
steps.guidance: Rest. There was nothing to achieve — only to notice.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: If your partner is present, agree this is pressure-free. If not, rehearse it in your mind.

steps.title: Attend
steps.guidance: Slow, curious touch with no goal of performance. Focus on giving and receiving sensation.

steps.title: Close
steps.guidance: Rest together or reflect. Connection over outcome.

steps.title: Understand
steps.guidance: Overstimulation can dull arousal. This is about rebalancing, not shame.

steps.title: Reflect
steps.guidance: Bring to mind real intimacy — warmth, touch, presence. Let that be the cue, not a screen.

steps.title: Commit
steps.guidance: Choose one small change for today. Small and repeated wins.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: On demand — no warm-up
steps.guidance: Real moments don't wait for you to get ready. Right now, cold, engage the floor — gently, cleanly. And release, fully. Again, no ramp-up. Engage... release. Quick to answer, clean to let go. A few of these at random intervals, then a couple of flicks and one long hold just to keep things ticking.

steps.title: Presence — control without bracing
steps.guidance: The skill that counts when it's real. Engage the floor gently — and at the same time check the rest of you: jaw loose, shoulders down, breath easy. Control in one place, relaxed everywhere else. That combination — calm body, gentle control — is worth more than any strong squeeze. Hold it a moment... and let go. Out in your life, use it in the moments that matter; the everyday steadiness and the calm are the whole point.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Relax
steps.guidance: Settle into calm. Slow breath.

steps.title: Rehearse
steps.guidance: Picture a calm, confident encounter from start to finish. Feel steadiness, not pressure.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Carry that steady feeling with you. You’ve practised it.

steps.title: Why it helps
steps.guidance: Open talk lowers performance pressure more than any technique.

steps.title: Rehearse a line
steps.guidance: Plan one honest, kind sentence you could say to your partner about going slow. Practise it in your mind.

steps.title: Commit
steps.guidance: Decide when you might share it. No rush.

steps.title: Reframe
steps.guidance: A first or next encounter is an experience to share, not a test to pass.

steps.title: Rehearse calm
steps.guidance: Picture yourself relaxed, present, and unhurried. Breathe into that image.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Confidence is built in small reps like this one.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: The other direction
steps.guidance: Gentle only — a soft letting-go, never a hard push. On the in-breath, as the belly expands, let the floor lengthen and soften downward, as if quietly opening. On the out-breath, return to soft neutral. The feeling is subtle, a gentle downward release. No pushing, no force, keep breathing. If anything hurts or feels like pressure, stop.

steps.title: Lengthen and rest
steps.guidance: Lengthen the floor and stay there for a couple of slow breaths — resting at the open, soft end of the range. Then ease back to neutral. This is the half of control most programs skip: being able to fully let go, on purpose.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Frame
steps.guidance: Arousal follows calm, not pressure. We're building ease in the body, not chasing a performance.

steps.title: Breathe into it
steps.guidance: Slow, low breaths. As the body settles, things have room to respond naturally. Nothing to push.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Recall a moment you felt easy and at home in your body. Let that steadiness be your baseline.

steps.title: Normalise
steps.guidance: A first time, or a return after a long gap, deserves patience. Nerves are normal — they are not a verdict on you.

steps.title: Rehearse calm
steps.guidance: Picture the moment unfolding slowly. Pauses are allowed. Breathing is allowed. Ease over urgency.

steps.title: One intention
steps.guidance: Set a single gentle intention: presence over performance.

steps.title: Notice
steps.guidance: Notice the pull toward a screen without judging it. Awareness is the first lever, not willpower.

steps.title: Redirect
steps.guidance: When the urge rises, breathe and bring to mind real connection — warmth, touch, presence. Let that be the cue.

steps.title: Small win
steps.guidance: Choose one screen-free choice today. Small, repeated wins rewire the pattern over time.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Full-length holds
steps.guidance: Strength at its peak: lift small, hold for about 8 to 10 seconds, breathing the whole way, then lower in control — never a drop, rest as long as you held. Keep the lift small; if you can't breathe through it, it's too big. A few rounds, generous rest.

steps.title: Hold and pulse
steps.guidance: Keep the combo sharp: a steady base hold with a few quick pulses on top, the base never dropping, then a slow controlled descent. About three, with good rests.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit tall but easy. Let the shoulders drop.

steps.title: Lengthen the out-breath
steps.guidance: Breathe in for a count of four, out for a count of six. The long exhale tells your body it is safe to relax.

steps.title: Rest
steps.guidance: Let breathing return to normal. Notice the calm you made.

steps.title: Connect
steps.guidance: Rest a hand low on your belly. Breathe slow and low, not high in the chest.

steps.title: Soften on the exhale
steps.guidance: As you breathe out, let the pelvic floor gently release downward. Breath and muscle, working together.

steps.title: Close
steps.guidance: No effort to hold anything. Just easy breath.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Slow holds
steps.guidance: First gear — slow holds. On the out-breath, lift small and clean. Hold gently for about 4 seconds, breathing through the hold. Release on the in-breath, all the way, and rest about 6 seconds. Belly, glutes and thighs stay soft — only the floor works. About six, no rush, full release every time.

steps.title: Quick flicks
steps.guidance: Second gear — quick flicks. Fast: squeeze and let go right away, like flicking a switch on then off. Don't time these to your breath — too quick for that. Just keep breathing normally. Even fast, the let-go has to be complete. Squeeze, full release. About eight. If it gets muddy after a few, stop there — the fast fibres tire quickly at first.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: The elevator
steps.guidance: We add control to the slow gear. Think of the lift like an elevator with a few floors. On the out-breath, lift gently to floor one — pause. A bit more, floor two — pause. Up to floor three, your comfortable max, still small, still breathing — hold a couple of seconds. Now the part that matters most: come back down in the same steps. Don't drop. Floor two... pause... floor one... all the way to the bottom, soft. Lowering slowly, in control, is its own skill — the opposite of gripping.

steps.title: Quick flicks
steps.guidance: Now the fast gear, unchanged. Crisp on, full off, breathing free, not synced. Two short rounds of about ten. Then try one round standing — same muscle, it feels a little different upright, and that's normal.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Meet them
steps.guidance: Two muscles at the base — the ischiocavernosus and bulbocavernosus — do much of the control work. No need to memorise the names.

steps.title: What they do
steps.guidance: They squeeze at the base to support firmness and influence the timing of release. Training them is what these sessions build.

steps.title: Why it helps
steps.guidance: Responsive muscles mean steadier control. Gentle, regular practice is the path.

steps.title: Two directions
steps.guidance: A healthy pelvic floor does two things well: it lifts (squeeze) and it lets go (release).

steps.title: Balance matters
steps.guidance: All squeezing and no releasing leaves muscles tight and tired. We train both, on purpose.

steps.title: Takeaway
steps.guidance: Strength and relaxation together — that is control.

steps.title: Set up
steps.guidance: Lie back, knees bent, body soft.

steps.title: Release on the exhale
steps.guidance: Breathe in slowly; on the long exhale, let the pelvic floor open and drop, like an easing of tension. No pushing.

steps.title: Notice
steps.guidance: That downward softening is the reverse kegel. This is the skill that builds control.

steps.title: Scan
steps.guidance: Move attention from jaw to shoulders to belly to pelvic floor. Notice where you hold.

steps.title: Let each go
steps.guidance: At each spot, soften on an out-breath. Tension stored in the body works against you here.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: End loose and easy.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Keep the strength ticking
steps.guidance: A short bit of lifting first to keep what you've built — a few easy endurance holds, about 5 seconds, lift small, breathe, lower in control. Just five or six. We're maintaining today, not pushing.

steps.title: Lengthening — gently
steps.guidance: Gentle only — this is a soft letting-go, never a hard push. If you're straining or bearing down with force, ease right off; it should feel like more relaxation, not effort. On the in-breath, as the belly expands, let the floor gently lengthen and soften downward, as if quietly opening. On the out-breath, return to soft neutral — not a lift. The feeling is subtle. No pushing, no force. If anything hurts or feels like pressure, stop.

steps.title: The contrast
steps.guidance: Now feel the whole range. Gently lift — a small kegel. Back to neutral. Now gently lengthen and open — down. Back to neutral. Up... neutral... down... neutral. Smooth, no force in either direction. Strength without the ability to fully let go isn't the goal — now your muscle can do both.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Peak endurance holds
steps.guidance: The holds reach their full length this week. Lift small, hold for about 8 to 10 seconds, breathing the whole way, then lower in control — never a drop. Rest as long as you held. About eight. The temptation at this length is to grip harder and hold your breath — don't. Keep the lift small; if you can't breathe through it, it's too big, so shorten the hold.

steps.title: Supple check
steps.guidance: Don't lose last week's work. A few gentle lengthens: in-breath, let the floor lengthen and open; out-breath, neutral. Gentle, no force. Just three or four, to keep the door open in both directions. Then out in your day, keep the floor gently present as you walk — engaged for a stretch, breathing normally, then let it go.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Frame
steps.guidance: Picture arousal as a ladder from one to ten. The skill is noticing the rungs.

steps.title: Ease at the edge
steps.guidance: As you sense the higher rungs, slow down, breathe, soften — let it settle a step before continuing.

steps.title: Learn
steps.guidance: Each time you ease back, you teach your body that you are in charge.

steps.title: Set up
steps.guidance: Get calm and unhurried.

steps.title: Insert the pause
steps.guidance: At the point of rising urgency, fully pause — three slow breaths — before going on. The pause is the practice.

steps.title: Carry it
steps.guidance: This same pause works in real moments.

steps.title: Notice the cue
steps.guidance: Spot what pulls you to a screen — boredom, stress, habit. Naming it loosens its grip.

steps.title: Swap the cue
steps.guidance: Decide one real alternative in advance: a walk, a call, a few breaths. Plan beats willpower.

steps.title: Commit
steps.guidance: Pick today's swap. One is enough.

steps.title: Watch it rise
steps.guidance: An urge is a wave — it builds, peaks, and falls if you let it.

steps.title: Ride, don't fight
steps.guidance: Breathe and observe the urge without acting. Notice it soften on its own.

steps.title: Note the win
steps.guidance: You did not have to obey it. That is the muscle you are building.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: The maintenance dose
steps.guidance: Keeping what you've built takes far less than building it did. A short run of the essentials is plenty: a few holds, a round of flicks, a couple of gentle lengthens. That's enough to hold what you've got — you don't need the full build phase forever.

steps.title: Anchoring
steps.guidance: The real skill is anchoring: tie a small dose to something you already do every day, so the cue does the remembering for you. Quick flicks while you brush your teeth. A couple of holds while the kettle boils. The knack every time you stand up. A gentle engage each time you walk through a doorway. Pick one and say it: 'every time I ____, I'll ____.' Over this week, settle on two or three anchors and the handful of elements you'll keep. That's your maintenance plan.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Keep it ticking
steps.guidance: First, a light maintenance run: a few holds, a round of flicks, a couple of gentle lengthens. Brief is fine — that's the point of maintenance.

steps.title: Three honest things
steps.guidance: Plateaus are normal — after the early gains, progress slows; that isn't failing, it's what training does. Don't grind harder; trust the maintenance. Falling off is recoverable — you'll miss days, maybe weeks, and that's nothing to feel bad about. When you come back, drop back a couple of weeks and rebuild gently; the muscle remembers and returns faster than it took to build. And the honest one: this helps a lot, but it doesn't fix everything. If you've practised consistently and something still isn't improving, or there's pain, or something feels off — that's a doctor, not more training. Seeing a doctor is the strong move. The warning-signs article walks through this carefully.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Picture it
steps.guidance: Imagine an intimate moment unfolding slowly and easily, with room to breathe.

steps.title: Stay with ease
steps.guidance: If tension creeps in, pause the scene, breathe, and restart calm. You are training the nervous system.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Calm rehearsed is calm available.

steps.title: Normalise
steps.guidance: Moments do not always go to plan. That is human, not failure.

steps.title: Rehearse the recovery
steps.guidance: Picture a stumble, then yourself staying warm and unbothered — a breath, a smile, carrying on. Recovery is a skill too.

steps.title: Close
steps.guidance: Kindness to yourself is part of the training.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Somewhere private and unhurried. Slow your breathing first.

steps.title: Touch with the breath
steps.guidance: Move slow, curious touch in time with your breath. No goal but to notice what each spot feels like.

steps.title: Rest
steps.guidance: Nothing to achieve. Just what you noticed.

steps.title: Begin
steps.guidance: Sit or lie comfortably, unhurried.

steps.title: Explore beyond the obvious
steps.guidance: Bring attention to hands, forearms, chest, neck — neutral curiosity, no destination. Sensation for its own sake.

steps.title: Close
steps.guidance: Let the attention soften and rest.

steps.title: Agree
steps.guidance: Agree it is pressure-free. If your partner is not here, rehearse it in your mind.

steps.title: Give, then receive
steps.guidance: One gives slow, curious touch; the other simply receives. Then swap. No goal of performance.

steps.title: Reflect
steps.guidance: Connection over outcome. Notice how that felt.

steps.title: Set the tone
steps.guidance: Pressure-free, unhurried, together or rehearsed.

steps.title: Say it out loud
steps.guidance: As you give or receive touch, name simply what feels pleasant. Words build presence and lower pressure.

steps.title: Close
steps.guidance: Rest together. Closeness, not a test.

steps.title: Reframe
steps.guidance: Slowing down or pausing is always allowed — in rehearsal and in real life.

steps.title: Rehearse the pause
steps.guidance: Picture a moment, then yourself calmly slowing, breathing, continuing when ready. The pause is yours to take.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Knowing you can pause is what lets you relax.

steps.title: Reframe
steps.guidance: An encounter is shared experience, not a result to deliver.

steps.title: Rest attention on connection
steps.guidance: Picture focusing on warmth, touch, and the other person — not on monitoring yourself.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Presence is the whole skill. Build it in small reps like this.

steps.title: Why it helps
steps.guidance: Communication is as much hearing them as speaking. Curiosity lowers pressure for both.

steps.title: Rehearse a question
steps.guidance: Plan one kind, open question you could ask your partner about what they enjoy or want to slow down.

steps.title: Commit
steps.guidance: Decide a calm moment to ask. No rush.

steps.title: Idea
steps.guidance: A gentle word afterward builds trust as much as anything beforehand.

steps.title: Rehearse a line
steps.guidance: Plan one warm, honest sentence for after — appreciation or a small reflection, not analysis.

steps.title: Commit
steps.guidance: Kindness, on both sides of the moment.

steps.title: Set up
steps.guidance: Lie back, knees bent, fully soft.

steps.title: Exhale-led release
steps.guidance: On each long out-breath, let the pelvic floor drop and open a little more. Releasing, never pushing.

steps.title: Notice
steps.guidance: This softening is the skill that gives you control.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Slow the breath; soften jaw and shoulders.

steps.title: Let go, calmly
steps.guidance: Picture an easy, unhurried moment as you release the pelvic floor on each exhale. Calm body, open muscles.

steps.title: Close
steps.guidance: Loose and easy. Well done.

steps.title: Relax in order
steps.guidance: Soften feet, legs, belly, chest, shoulders, jaw — one by one. A calm body is the foundation.

steps.title: Let it follow
steps.guidance: With the body at ease, things respond in their own time. Nothing to force or chase.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Ease first. The rest follows.

steps.title: Frame
steps.guidance: Pressure works against you here. Patience works with you.

steps.title: Breathe and wait
steps.guidance: Slow breaths, no monitoring, no rushing. Let things build at their own pace.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: Trust, not effort.

steps.title: Reframe
steps.guidance: Readiness is built in small, low-pressure reps — not one big leap.

steps.title: Picture one step
steps.guidance: Imagine a single, easy, low-stakes step forward and yourself calm through it.

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: One small step counts. You've taken one now.

steps.title: Notice the voice
steps.guidance: Spot the harsh inner line — 'what if I fail'. Naming it loosens it.

steps.title: Replace it
steps.guidance: Swap in something steady and true: 'I can go slow, I can pause, I am okay.'

steps.title: Anchor
steps.guidance: How you speak to yourself shapes how you show up.

steps.title: Sit with it
steps.guidance: Let a dull, restless moment exist without reaching for a screen. It passes.

steps.title: Breathe through
steps.guidance: Three slow breaths. Notice the restlessness rise and fade on its own.

steps.title: Note the win
steps.guidance: You did not need the screen. That is the rewire.

steps.title: Spot the reward
steps.guidance: Screens offer a fast, hollow reward. Real life offers slower, fuller ones.

steps.title: Choose one
steps.guidance: Pick one real reward for today — movement, a call, sunlight, rest — and plan when.

steps.title: Commit
steps.guidance: One real choice, repeated, retrains the pull.

steps.title: Warm up
steps.guidance: A few gentle squeezes with full releases.

steps.title: Ease with the exhale
steps.guidance: As arousal rises, use a long, slow out-breath to ease it back a step. Breath is your steadiest brake.

steps.title: Integrate
steps.guidance: Slow exhale, then continue. Usable anywhere.

steps.title: Map it
steps.guidance: Learn your own cues for rising arousal — the early rungs, not just the top.

steps.title: Ease earlier
steps.guidance: Practise easing back at an early rung — squeeze, breathe, soften — before urgency peaks. Earlier is easier.

steps.title: Integrate
steps.guidance: The sooner you notice, the more control you have.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Your routine
steps.guidance: Last week of the foundation. You know these now, so run them at your own pace: a few elevators — up in steps, slowly down, sitting. A round of quick flicks. Then the same, standing. Find your own rhythm. If you've landed on a version that feels right, trust it.

steps.title: The knack
steps.guidance: The knack is timing a contraction to a moment, before it happens, not after — a gentle lift just before you cough, or as you stand up. Sit forward. Just before you rise, lift the floor gently. Hold it as you stand. Let go once you're up. Anticipate... contract... hold through the moment... release after. Bringing it in ahead of the moment is what turns this from an exercise into control you can actually use.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: The full routine
steps.guidance: You have every piece now — today we run them as one routine. A few full-length endurance holds, 8 to 10 seconds, controlled down — about five, keeping your strength, not chasing more. Then hold-and-pulse: steady base, a few pulses on top, slow down — about three. Then quick flicks, crisp on and full off, about ten. And a supple check — a few gentle lengthens, in-breath open, out-breath neutral.

steps.title: Strong, quick, supple
steps.guidance: That's the whole thing — strong, quick, and supple in one short routine. Two pieces live in your day, not in here: the knack before a cough or standing up, and keeping the floor gently present as you move. And one idea that matters: you don't have to do all of this, all-out, every day to keep it. Steady and sustainable holds far better than hard and brittle.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Gentle lengthening
steps.guidance: If your floor runs tight, this is the work that helps most — and it's gentle by design. Never a forceful bearing-down. On the in-breath, let the floor lengthen and soften downward, as if quietly opening; on the out-breath, return to easy neutral. Slow, breath-led, fully relaxed, long pauses. If you're straining or red in the face, you've gone too far — ease right off. It should feel like more relaxation, not effort.

steps.title: Lengthen and rest
steps.guidance: Let the floor lengthen and stay there for a few slow breaths, resting at the soft, open end. Next time you notice you're clenched — jaw, shoulders, or down there — try a conscious lengthen and release, right then. That's down-training where it actually matters. Please get this looked at by a doctor or pelvic-floor physiotherapist when you can.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Find the whole range
steps.guidance: Gentle only, no force in either direction. Gently lift a small kegel, back to neutral; now gently lengthen and open downward, back to neutral. Up... neutral... down... neutral, smooth and easy. Spend most of your time at the lengthening end — that's the side that needs the practice. Keep breathing; stop if anything hurts.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.

steps.title: Settle
steps.guidance: Sit however you're comfortable — a chair, the edge of a bed. Let your shoulders drop. Rest one hand on your belly. Breathe in slowly through your nose, and let the belly rise into your hand — not the chest. Now let it out, slow and long, like a quiet sigh. Again. In, the belly rises... out, longer than the in. With each out-breath, let a little more go — your jaw, your shoulders, the area between your sit bones.

steps.title: Run your routine
steps.guidance: Last week of the program — not the last week of the practice. I'm not going to call the parts this time; you don't need me to. Whatever shape it's become — the full version or your maintenance plan — run it yourself, start to finish. Take the whole block.

steps.title: Yours now
steps.guidance: That's the thing you'll carry out of here — it's yours now, completely. The program ends in a few days; the practice doesn't. You've got your plan, your anchors, your gentle way back if you ever fall off, and the sense to know when something's a doctor's job. That's a complete set. You're ready to keep this without a program telling you to.

steps.title: Down-regulate
steps.guidance: Stop lifting now. Let everything down there go heavy and soft — more relaxed than when you started. A few more slow breaths, long out-breaths. Nothing to do here, nothing to achieve. Just sitting, breathing, calm. That's the session.
